mismatch_files.summary: Description claims to test interactive-mode output_stream_t resource cleanup (issue #1163), but the test harness runs each block as a separate process, making that impossible — the tests actually just verify balance calculations with formatted output at various date cutoffs.

issues.description_says: Test for issue #1163: Interactive mode crash after multiple commands — output_stream_t resource cleanup preventing crashes after 3-10 commands
issues.actually_tests: Basic balance commands with formatted output at different date cutoffs; each test block is a separate ledger process and cannot test interactive/multi-command resource cleanup

issues.description_says: Run multiple balance commands with format options similar to the crash scenario; each command should properly initialize and clean up the output stream
issues.actually_tests: Independent balance queries against separate ledger invocations — the test harness never runs multiple commands in a single session, so stream cleanup between commands is untestable here

mismatch_files.summary: Scenario 1 comment says 'net 6ea' will remain, but the single test block covers all three transactions and verifies 4ea (after Scenario 2 removes another 2ea).

issues.description_says: the net 6ea should be left in the account
issues.actually_tests: the test verifies 4ea remaining (10 - 4 from Scenario 1 - 2 from Scenario 2 = 4ea), not 6ea

mismatch_files.summary: Comments claim this tests Python interpreter stability, but the test block is a plain `reg` command with no Python integration.

issues.description_says: Python interpreter should not segfault on second command
issues.actually_tests: A plain `reg` command with no Python involvement whatsoever

issues.description_says: Tests basic python integration stability by running a simple ledger query through the python interface
issues.actually_tests: Tests a standard register report output with two postings — no Python interface used

mismatch_files.summary: The comments describe a crash-from-deleted-cwd scenario and a safe_current_path() fix, but the test only runs a trivial balance query that has no connection to the described bug; the actual crash scenario is never exercised.

issues.description_says: Regression test for crash when run from a non-existent (deleted) directory — fix adds safe_current_path() that catches error_code and falls back to '/'
issues.actually_tests: A basic `bal Assets` command on a normal journal file with no attempt to simulate a deleted or unavailable working directory

mismatch_files.summary: Description claims total lot-price notation is always preserved, but expected output converts {{100.00 GBP}} (100 units) to {1.00 GBP} per-unit for the second lot while preserving {{90.00 GBP}} for the first

issues.description_says: The print command should preserve {{total}} lot-price notation rather than converting it to {per-unit} notation
issues.actually_tests: The expected output preserves {{90.00 GBP}} for the first lot, but converts {{100.00 GBP}} to {1.00 GBP} (per-unit) for the second lot — contradicting the stated invariant

mismatch_files.summary: Header claims to test automatic FIFO/LIFO lot matching when selling, but the test contains only buy transactions and verifies lot display ordering in balance output — no lot consumption or sell-side matching occurs

issues.description_says: Test automatic FIFO/LIFO lot matching when selling without explicit lot annotation
issues.actually_tests: Display ordering of lots using --lots-fifo and --lots-lifo balance options; there are no sell transactions in the file at all

mismatch_files.summary: Comments describe a Python crash bug fix (scope parent linkage) but the test contains no Python test blocks and only exercises routine print/reg/bal formatting on a BTC transaction.

issues.description_says: Test documents issue #1764: Crash with Python extensions — Python-created postings causing segfault during expression evaluation due to missing scope parent linkage
issues.actually_tests: Basic print/reg/bal output of a BTC transaction with total price (@@) syntax — no Python test blocks exist anywhere in the file

issues.description_says: Since Python bindings may not be available, this test validates basic posting operations work correctly with the new scope parent linkage
issues.actually_tests: Standard ledger output formatting for a BTC/USD transaction; nothing specifically exercises scope parent linkage or distinguishes Python-created vs C++-created postings

mismatch_files.summary: Test 3 comment incorrectly calls the search pattern 'accented' when '@Resume' is plain ASCII matching accented payee 'Résumé'; should say 'ASCII pattern matching accented payee' like Test 1's description.

issues.description_says: Payee search with accented pattern matching accented payee
issues.actually_tests: ASCII pattern '@Resume' (no accents) matching accented payee 'Résumé Services' — the search term is plain ASCII, not accented

mismatch_files.summary: Comments claim the test covers --depth for both register and balance reports, but only balance (bal) tests are present; no register test block exists

issues.description_says: Fixed by PR #1901: Make --depth in register report collapse sub-accounts
issues.actually_tests: Only balance report (bal) with --depth is tested; no register (reg) test blocks exist

issues.description_says: --depth for register and balance reports
issues.actually_tests: Only --depth for balance report; no register test block present

mismatch_files.summary: Comment says the test exercises `ledger print` but the test block uses `bal`

issues.description_says: UTF-8 encoding should be preserved in ledger print
issues.actually_tests: UTF-8 encoding in the `bal` (balance) command, not `print`

mismatch_files.summary: Header comments claim two quoted-commodity-name scenarios are tested, but the file only contains one test for the unquoted case; the inline comment before the test block is accurate, but items 1 and 2 in the header description are misleading.

issues.description_says: Tests that a *quoted* commodity name with digits ('CTG25') works correctly in a format directive when it matches the commodity name
issues.actually_tests: There is no test for a quoted commodity name — both tests are absent; the file only tests the unquoted case where 'CTG25' is silently truncated to 'CTG'

issues.description_says: Tests that a mismatched *quoted* commodity name with digits produces a clear error message naming the full symbol
issues.actually_tests: The single test block uses an unquoted 'CTG25' in a format directive for commodity 'CTG', verifying the parse error about unexpected characters '25'. No quoted-commodity scenario is tested.

mismatch_files.summary: Comments describe a cost-rounding test (dollar amounts), but the test block checks commodity quantity (2.00 bread), not any dollar cost; cost rounding is only tested implicitly via transaction balance validation (not an explicit assertion)

issues.description_says: per-unit price annotations (@) must round each posting's cost to the commodity's precision independently before summing into the balance
issues.actually_tests: the balance of the Expenses account shows 2.00 bread (0.50 + 1.50), which is just a commodity quantity sum with no dollar amounts in the output

issues.description_says: test validates cost rounding: $1.995 rounds to $2.00, $7.485 rounds to $7.49, sum is $9.49
issues.actually_tests: bal --flat --no-total Expenses produces '2.00 bread  Expenses' — verifying commodity quantity arithmetic (0.50+1.50=2.00), not dollar cost rounding

mismatch_files.summary: Header claims to test null-pointer crash handling for missing arguments, but all tests use valid syntax with a proper argument; the crash/null-pointer scenario is never exercised

issues.description_says: Test verifies apply_tag_directive properly handles missing arguments and doesn't crash with a null pointer dereference
issues.actually_tests: Only tests valid usage: 'apply tag Important' with a proper argument — no test for the crash scenario (missing/null argument)

issues.description_says: Verify that the tag was applied
issues.actually_tests: Runs 'bal --no-total' which shows account balances; does not verify the tag 'Important' was actually attached to the transaction (would need --format %(tag('Important')) or similar)

mismatch_files.summary: `--lots` and `--lots-lifo` expected outputs are identical, contradicting the comment that the data was designed to make all orderings distinguishable.

issues.description_says: price order is the inverse of date order to ensure the two orderings are distinguishable
issues.actually_tests: The `--lots` and `--lots-lifo` test blocks have identical expected output ($100/$150/$200 order), because `--lots` sorts by price ascending which equals date descending (LIFO) in this dataset. Only `--lots-fifo` produces a distinct ordering; the data does NOT make all three orderings distinguishable.

mismatch_files.summary: Header comment promises four behaviors including error-case testing, but the file only implements two success-case tests (exit code 0); no invalid-file test exists.

issues.description_says: 4. Return a non-zero exit code and report errors for an invalid file
issues.actually_tests: The file contains only two tests, both using a valid journal file and both expecting exit code 0. There is no test block that exercises an invalid file or a non-zero exit code.

mismatch_files.summary: Comment references `!include` directive but the test uses `include`; both are functionally equivalent in Ledger, so the described behavior (case-sensitive file inclusion failing with an error) is otherwise accurately captured.

issues.description_says: !include should be case-sensitive
issues.actually_tests: The test uses `include` (without the `!` prefix), not `!include`

mismatch_files.summary: Terminology mismatch: 'deprecation'/'Deprecate' used throughout but the test exercises accounting depreciation; all three test blocks and their expected outputs are arithmetically correct and accurately described.

issues.description_says: Simulate asset **deprecation** using effective dates
issues.actually_tests: Asset **depreciation** (accounting amortization of asset cost over time) — 'deprecation' is a software term meaning 'mark as obsolete', not the accounting concept of reducing book value

issues.description_says: Transaction payee: 'Deprecate instruments'
issues.actually_tests: The transaction records accounting **depreciation** (spreading asset cost reduction across time periods via effective dates), not deprecation in the software sense

mismatch_files.summary: Minor factual error in day-of-week annotation: the comment '; mon' on 2003/09/14 should be '; sun' (September 14, 2003 was a Sunday); the test itself correctly exercises 'every 12 months' period grouping and the expected output is accurate

issues.description_says: mon (Monday)
issues.actually_tests: 2003/09/14 was a Sunday — Sept 13 was Saturday, so Sept 14 must be Sunday, not Monday

mismatch_files.summary: The description references the Emacs 'ledger-post-align-xact' function but tests CLI commands, and more critically, the 'print' test expected output incorrectly preserves the expression '($25.00 + $25.00)' rather than its evaluated value '$50.00', which would cause the test to fail.

issues.description_says: Test for issue #1175: ledger-post-align-xact does not correctly align lines with balance assignments or expression amounts
issues.actually_tests: The CLI 'print' and 'xact' commands, not the Emacs ledger-mode 'ledger-post-align-xact' function. While ledger-mode calls these CLI commands internally, the test exercises the CLI tools directly, not the Emacs Lisp alignment function named in the description.

issues.description_says: Expression Amount transaction with ($25.00 + $25.00) preserved as-is in print output
issues.actually_tests: The 'print' command evaluates arithmetic expressions at parse time, so ($25.00 + $25.00) would be output as '$50.00', not '($25.00 + $25.00)'. The expected output in the test block (lines 27-44) shows the expression literal preserved, which would not match actual 'print' output — meaning this test would fail.

issues.description_says: ; Test the xact command which is used by ledger-mode
issues.actually_tests: That 'xact --now=2026/02/16 Balance' produces a draft transaction matching 'Balance Assignment'. The comment describes ledger-mode usage context but doesn't explain what alignment aspect is actually being verified (handling of balance assignment syntax '= $150.00' in xact output).

mismatch_files.summary: The regression test claims to verify that lot dates are not replaced by transaction dates, but uses identical values for both, making the test unable to detect the bug it claims to prevent.

issues.description_says: lot date should be preserved, not replaced by the transaction date during finalization
issues.actually_tests: both transactions use lot dates that are identical to their transaction dates ([2020/10/01] on 2020/10/01 and [2020/11/01] on 2020/11/01), so the test cannot distinguish between 'lot date preserved' and 'lot date replaced by transaction date' — either behavior produces the same output

mismatch_files.summary: Lines 10-12 claim 'no unrealized gains or losses' but the reg --market test verifies a $-450 revaluation entry exists; only the balance nets to zero, not the register

issues.description_says: There should be no unrealized gains or losses (after the stock split, same total value $500)
issues.actually_tests: The reg --market test explicitly shows a $-450 'Commodities revalued' revaluation entry on 2004-05-02, which IS an unrealized gain/loss entry in the register output

mismatch_files.summary: Comment lists four comparison operators (>=, <=, >, <) but the test only uses two (>= and <); the Y directive and date-range filtering logic are otherwise accurately described.

issues.description_says: Date comparison operators (>=, <=, >, <) in automated transaction predicates should work correctly
issues.actually_tests: Only >= and < operators are used in the automated transaction predicate (d>=[2014/01/01] & d<[2015/01/01]); <= and > are not exercised

mismatch_files.summary: Line 21 incorrectly claims the buggy /. / regex 'would still match' account names, when in fact standard account names have no spaces so the test would produce empty output (clearly failing); also, %foo is described as matching posting-level tags but actually matches via transaction-level metadata.

issues.description_says: Without the fix, the dot would include a trailing space, creating /. / which would still match but reveals the parsing was wrong
issues.actually_tests: Standard ledger account names (Expenses:Food, Assets:Checking) contain no spaces, so the regex /. / (any-char + space) would NOT match them at all. Without the fix, the output would be empty, causing the test to clearly fail — not 'still match'

issues.description_says: %foo should match only postings with the 'foo' metadata tag
issues.actually_tests: The metadata '; foo: bar' at line 9 is transaction-level (appears before any posting), so %foo matches ALL postings in that transaction — including Assets:Checking which has no foo tag. The expected output confirms this by showing both postings.

mismatch_files.summary: Header claims both 'v' and 'g' are unsupported in ledger 3.x, but only 'v' is tested for error behavior; 'g' has no corresponding error test and the actual error message only mentions V/v — suggesting 'g' may still be available or was never blocked

issues.description_says: Both 'v' (market value) and 'g' (gain) from ledger 2.x are no longer supported
issues.actually_tests: Only 'v' is tested for producing an error (exit code 1); 'g' is never tested as unsupported — Test 3 only tests the *replacement formula* 'market(amount) - cost', not that 'g' itself is deprecated or errors

issues.description_says: Error message implies both V/v and g are removed
issues.actually_tests: The expected error message says only 'The V and v value expression variables are no longer supported' — no mention of 'g', which suggests 'g' was never blocked in the source code, contradicting the header comment

mismatch_files.summary: Comment claims the test is specifically about fixed lot price {=price} notation, but Transaction 2 uses a regular lot cost {$1.5766} without the '=' prefix — the test exercises both fixed and non-fixed lot price annotations, making the description incomplete

issues.description_says: When using a fixed lot price {=price}, the annotated commodity should not appear as a distinct commodity
issues.actually_tests: Transaction 2 uses a regular lot cost annotation {$1.5766} (no '=' prefix), not a fixed lot price — only Transaction 1 uses the fixed-price {=$1.3866} syntax described in the comment

mismatch_files.summary: Comment 'verify not works with payee queries too' precedes the positive payee test (no 'not'), while the negative test that actually validates 'not' behavior is uncommented

issues.description_says: Payee filter: verify 'not' works with payee queries too
issues.actually_tests: test print payee Pension — a positive payee filter with no 'not'; the test that actually uses 'not' with payee (line 51) has no comment

mismatch_files.summary: The only comment in the file ('# This needs to be updated') is a stale developer TODO note, not a description of what the biweekly periodic transaction exercises; it misrepresents the test as incomplete when it is a committed regression test.

issues.description_says: # This needs to be updated (implying the test or biweekly transaction is incomplete/a TODO)
issues.actually_tests: A committed regression test for issue #1858 involving automated transactions, periodic budgets, and balance output — there is nothing tentative about this test; it runs and produces defined output

mismatch_files.summary: The round() test does not demonstrate any rounding (output equals input), undermining its stated purpose; the PR reference (#96) appears anachronistically low relative to the issue number (#634).

issues.description_says: Fixed by PR #96
issues.actually_tests: GitHub issues and PRs share sequential numbering; PR #96 would predate issue #634 by hundreds of entries, making it highly unlikely PR #96 fixed issue #634. The PR reference appears incorrect.

issues.description_says: round() correctly implemented (per header comment 'Correctly implement floor(), ceil() and round()')
issues.actually_tests: The round() test uses $100.567 which establishes commodity precision at 3 decimal places; round($100.567) returns $100.567 unchanged — no actual rounding occurs. The test does not demonstrate round() rounding anything, making it useless as verification that round() works correctly. A meaningful test would use an amount with more decimal places than the commodity precision.

mismatch_files.summary: Description claims this tests ledgerrc/config-file short-option handling, but -S d is placed in the journal data section and the test exercises journal-file parsing, not config-file parsing

issues.description_says: Short options in ledgerrc should give clear error message — testing config file behavior
issues.actually_tests: The -S d appears in the journal data section of the .test file; ledger encounters it while parsing a journal/data file (via $FILE), not a ledgerrc or config file

issues.description_says: Short options like -S should not be supported in config files
issues.actually_tests: The test file is used as a journal input file (-f $FILE), so ledger parses -S d as unexpected journal content, not as a config file directive

mismatch_files.summary: Second scenario comment claims to verify equivalence of quoted/unquoted alias forms but only exercises one direction (quoted directive, unquoted transaction usage); primary bug-fix description and test design are accurate.

issues.description_says: verify that quoted and unquoted alias forms are equivalent when the commodity symbol does not end in a digit
issues.actually_tests: only tests that an alias defined with 'alias "BBBX"' (quoted directive) resolves when used as unquoted 'BBBX' in a transaction — does not test the reverse (alias BBBX unquoted directive with '"BBBX"' in transaction), so equivalence of both forms is not actually demonstrated
